evidence_passages.title: USS Dour (AM-223)
evidence_passages.text: USS "Dour" (AM-223) was an built for the United States Navy during World War II.

evidence_passages.title: USS Dour (AM-223)
evidence_passages.text:
USS Dour (AM-223)
USS "Dour" (AM-223) was an built for the United States Navy during World War II. She was awarded three battle stars for service in the Pacific during World War II. She was decommissioned in March 1947 and placed in reserve. While she remained in reserve, "Dour" was reclassified as MSF-223 in February 1955 but never reactivated. In October 1962, she was sold to the Mexican Navy and renamed ARM "DM-16". She was stricken from Mexican Navy service in 1986, but her ultimate fate is not reported in secondary sources.

evidence_passages.title: United States Navy SEALs
evidence_passages.text: The United States Navy's ``Sea, Air, and Land ''Teams, commonly abbreviated as the Navy SEALs, are the U.S.

evidence_passages.title: United States Navy SEALs
evidence_passages.text:
United States Navy SEALs
The Navy needed to determine its role within the special operations arena. In March 1961, Admiral Arleigh Burke, the Chief of Naval Operations, recommended the establishment of guerrilla and counter-guerrilla units. These units would be able to operate from sea, air or land. This was the beginning of the Navy SEALs. All SEALs came from the Navy's Underwater Demolition Teams, who had already gained extensive experience in commando warfare in Korea; however, the Underwater Demolition Teams were still necessary to the Navy's amphibious force.

ranked_passages.title: USS Dour (AM-223)
ranked_passages.text:
USS Dour (AM-223)
USS "Dour" (AM-223) was an built for the United States Navy during World War II. She was awarded three battle stars for service in the Pacific during World War II. She was decommissioned in March 1947 and placed in reserve. While she remained in reserve, "Dour" was reclassified as MSF-223 in February 1955 but never reactivated. In October 1962, she was sold to the Mexican Navy and renamed ARM "DM-16". She was stricken from Mexican Navy service in 1986, but her ultimate fate is not reported in secondary sources.

ranked_passages.title: United States Navy SEALs
ranked_passages.text:
United States Navy SEALs
The Navy needed to determine its role within the special operations arena. In March 1961, Admiral Arleigh Burke, the Chief of Naval Operations, recommended the establishment of guerrilla and counter-guerrilla units. These units would be able to operate from sea, air or land. This was the beginning of the Navy SEALs. All SEALs came from the Navy's Underwater Demolition Teams, who had already gained extensive experience in commando warfare in Korea; however, the Underwater Demolition Teams were still necessary to the Navy's amphibious force.

ranked_passages.title: Rudy Boesch
ranked_passages.text:
Rudy Boesch
Boesch was born and raised in Rochester, New York, and enlisted in the United States Navy at age 17. He became a Underwater Demolition Team (UDT) Frogman in 1951, serving on two UDT Teams. He was chosen as one of the first SEALs, becoming Chief of the Boat of newly created SEAL Team TWO in 1962. Starting in 1968 and 1970, Boesch completed two combat deployments during the Vietnam War, where he earned the Bronze Star for heroic action. During that time and later, Boesch set physical and operational standards at SEAL Team TWO. In 1987, he became Senior Enlisted Advisor for United States Special Operations Command. Designated the "Bullfrog", the longest-serving SEAL still on active duty, Boesch achieved considerable renown within the force for his physical fitness training regimens and his military appearance. After 45 years of continuous service, he retired from the Navy in 1990 as a Master Chief Petty Officer.

ranked_passages.title: USS Brownson (DD-868)
ranked_passages.text:
USS Brownson (DD-868)
USS "Brownson" (DD-868), a "Gearing"-class destroyer, was the second ship of the United States Navy to be named for Rear Admiral Willard H. Brownson, USN (1845–1935).

ranked_passages.title: USS Catbird (AM-68)
ranked_passages.text:
USS Catbird (AM-68)
USS "Catbird" (AM-68) was the lead ship of her class of two naval trawlers, which were operated as minesweepers by the United States Navy during World War II.

ranked_passages.title: California Golden Seals
ranked_passages.text:
California Golden Seals
The California Golden Seals were a team in the National Hockey League (NHL) from 1967 to 1976. Initially named California Seals, the team was renamed Oakland Seals partway through the 1967 -- 68 season (on December 8, 1967), and then to California Golden Seals in 1970. The Seals were one of six teams added to the league as part of the 1967 NHL expansion. Based in Oakland, California, they played their home games at the Oakland -- Alameda County Coliseum Arena. However, the Seals were never successful at the gate, and eventually moved to Cleveland to become the Cleveland Barons in 1976.

ranked_passages.title: USS Rhodes (DE-384)
ranked_passages.text:
USS Rhodes (DE-384)
USS "Rhodes" (DE-384) was an "Edsall"-class destroyer escort built for the United States Navy during World War II. She served in the Atlantic Ocean and the Pacific Ocean and provided destroyer escort protection against submarine and air attack for Navy vessels and convoys. Post-war she served the Navy as a radar picket ship.

ranked_passages.title: John W. Nyquist
ranked_passages.text:
John W. Nyquist
John Walfrid Nyquist (born February 11, 1933) is a retired a vice admiral in the United States Navy. He was born in San Diego, California, the son of rear admiral Nels Walfrid Nyquist and Irma Beske. He attended the University of Minnesota and United States Naval Academy, graduating from the latter in 1955. He is a former commander of Destroyer Squadron 26, Destroyer Squadron 20, USS "Semmes" (DDG-18) and Cruiser-Destroyer Group Five. He also worked in the Office of the Chief of Naval Operations as Assistant Deputy Chief of Naval Operations, Surface Warfare, and Assistant Chief of Naval Operations, Surface Warfare. He retired in 1991. His awards include the Distinguished Service Medal, the Legion of Merit, the Bronze Star, the Meritorious Service Medal, the Navy Commendation Medal two Meritorious Unit Commendations. In retirement he worked for Trex Enterprises Corporation, and is currently a defense consultant. He resides in Coronado, California, with his wife Penelope Ann Lyon.

ranked_passages.title: USS Goff (DD-247)
ranked_passages.text:
USS Goff (DD-247)
USS "Goff" (DD-247) was a "Clemson"-class destroyer in the United States Navy during World War II. She was named for Secretary of the Navy Nathan Goff, Jr.

ranked_passages.title: USS Macdonough (DD-351)
ranked_passages.text:
USS Macdonough (DD-351)
The third USS "Macdonough" (DD-351) was a "Farragut"-class destroyer in the United States Navy during World War II. She was named for Thomas Macdonough.

chunk.text:
United States Navy SEALs
The Navy needed to determine its role within the special operations arena. In March 1961, Admiral Arleigh Burke, the Chief of Naval Operations, recommended the establishment of guerrilla and counter-guerrilla units. These units would be able to operate from sea, air or land. This was the beginning of the Navy SEALs. All SEALs came from the Navy's Underwater Demolition Teams, who had already gained extensive experience in commando warfare in Korea; however, the Underwater Demolition Teams were still necessary to the Navy's amphibious force.

USS Dour (AM-223)
USS "Dour" (AM-223) was an built for the United States Navy during World War II. She was awarded three battle stars for service in the Pacific during World War II. She was decommissioned in March 1947 and placed in reserve. While she remained in reserve, "Dour" was reclassified as MSF-223 in February 1955 but never reactivated. In October 1962, she was sold to the Mexican Navy and renamed ARM "DM-16". She was stricken from Mexican Navy service in 1986, but her ultimate fate is not reported in secondary sources.

Rudy Boesch
Boesch was born and raised in Rochester, New York, and enlisted in the United States Navy at age 17. He became a Underwater Demolition Team (UDT) Frogman in 1951, serving on two UDT Teams. He was chosen as one of the first SEALs, becoming Chief of the Boat of newly created SEAL Team TWO in 1962. Starting in 1968 and 1970, Boesch completed two combat deployments during the Vietnam War, where he earned the Bronze Star for heroic action. During that time and later, Boesch set physical and operational standards at SEAL Team TWO. In 1987, he became Senior Enlisted Advisor for United States Special Operations Command. Designated the "Bullfrog", the longest-serving SEAL still on active duty, Boesch achieved considerable renown within the force for his physical fitness training regimens and his military appearance. After 45 years of continuous service, he retired from the Navy in 1990 as a Master Chief Petty Officer.

USS Catbird (AM-68)
USS "Catbird" (AM-68) was the lead ship of her class of two naval trawlers, which were operated as minesweepers by the United States Navy during World War II.

The Last Horse (Spanish:El ultimo caballo) is a 1950 Spanish comedy film directed by Edgar Neville starring Fernando Fernan Gomez.

The University of Southampton, which was founded in 1862 and received its Royal Charter as a university in 1952, has over 22,000 students. The university is ranked in the top 100 research universities in the world.

Question: When was Neville A. Stanton's employer founded?

Neville A. Stanton's employer is the University of Southampton. The University of Southampton was founded in 1862.
Answer: 1862

Wikipedia Title: USS Rhodes (DE-384)
USS Rhodes (DE-384) USS "Rhodes" (DE-384) was an "Edsall"-class destroyer escort built for the United States Navy during World War II. She served in the Atlantic Ocean and the Pacific Ocean and provided destroyer escort protection against submarine and air attack for Navy vessels and convoys.

Wikipedia Title: USS Balch (DD-363)
USS Balch (DD-363) USS "Balch" (DD-363) was a "Porter"-class destroyer in the United States Navy. She is named for Admiral George Beall Balch.

Wikipedia Title: USS Kilty (DD-137)
USS Kilty (DD-137) USS "Kilty" (DD–137) was a "Wickes"-class destroyer in the United States Navy. She was the first ship named for Admiral Augustus Kilty.

Wikipedia Title: USS MacLeish (DD-220)
USS MacLeish (DD-220) USS "MacLeish" (DD-220/AG-87) was a "Clemson"-class destroyer in the United States Navy during World War II. She was named for Lieutenant Kenneth MacLeish.

Wikipedia Title: USS Brownson (DD-868)
USS Brownson (DD-868) USS "Brownson" (DD-868), a "Gearing"-class destroyer, was the second ship of the United States Navy to be named for Rear Admiral Willard H. Brownson, USN (1845–1935).

Wikipedia Title: USS Catbird (AM-68)
USS Catbird (AM-68) USS "Catbird" (AM-68) was the lead ship of her class of two naval trawlers, which were operated as minesweepers by the United States Navy during World War II.

Wikipedia Title: Rudy Boesch
Rudy Boesch Boesch was born and raised in Rochester, New York, and enlisted in the United States Navy at age 17. In 1987, he became Senior Enlisted Advisor for United States Special Operations Command.

Question: What does SEAL stand for in the military group that is part of the military branch that operates USS Dour?
Thought:

qa_thought: The military branch that operates USS Dour is the United States Navy. The military group that is part of the United States Navy and includes SEALs is the Navy SEALs. SEAL stands for Sea, Air, and Land Teams.
qa_raw_response:
The military branch that operates USS Dour is the United States Navy. The military group that is part of the United States Navy and includes SEALs is the Navy SEALs. SEAL stands for Sea, Air, and Land Teams.
Answer: Sea, Air, and Land Teams
